era_context: The mid-8th century CE is marked by major political upheavals: the An Lushan Rebellion (755–763 CE) reaches a critical point with the death of its leader, the Abbasid Caliphate consolidates power following the 750 CE revolution, and the Frankish Kingdom expands under Pepin III. This is a year of significant military and political transitions across Eurasia.

events.title: Death of An Lushan; Rebellion Continues under An Qingxu
events.description: An Lushan, the Tang military governor whose rebellion began in 755 CE, dies in 757 CE (some sources date this to late 756 CE). His son An Qingxu assumes leadership of the rebellion, which continues to devastate Tang territories. The death of An Lushan does not end the rebellion but marks a transition in its leadership. The Tang Dynasty, under Emperor Suzong, continues to struggle against the rebellion with the aid of Uyghur allies.
key_figures: An Qingxu (son of An Lushan, new rebel leader)

sources.name: New Book of Tang (Xin Tang Shu)
Old Book of Tang (Jiu Tang Shu)
Zizhi Tongjian (Comprehensive Mirror for Aid in Government), compiled 11th century

events.certainty_note: An Lushan's death is firmly dated to 757 CE in Chinese chronicles, though some sources place it in late 756 CE. The date 757 CE is the scholarly consensus.

events.title: Abbasid Caliphate Suppresses Umayyad Loyalists; Administrative Reforms
events.description: The Abbasid Caliphate under al-Mansur (r. 754–775 CE) continues to consolidate power and suppress remaining Umayyad loyalists. This period sees the execution or exile of prominent Umayyad supporters and the integration of Persian administrative practices into the Islamic state. The Abbasid state is establishing itself as a more centralized, bureaucratic empire.

sources.name: Tabari, History of the Prophets and Kings

events.certainty_note: Abbasid consolidation is documented in later Islamic historiography, but specific events in 757 CE are often undated or conflated with broader trends.

events.title: Frankish Expansion under Pepin III
events.description: Pepin III continues military campaigns to expand Frankish power and consolidate control over neighboring territories. Campaigns against Aquitaine, Saxony, and other regions are conducted to extend Frankish hegemony. These military operations establish the foundation for Charlemagne's later expansionist campaigns.
events.certainty_note: Frankish military campaigns are documented in Carolingian-era chronicles, but specific campaigns in 757 CE are often undated or conflated with broader patterns of expansion.

events.title: Byzantine-Arab Naval Conflicts Continue
events.description: The Byzantine Empire under Constantine V continues naval warfare against Arab forces in the Mediterranean. Byzantine fleets engage in raids and defensive operations against Arab naval power. These conflicts are part of the broader struggle for control of Mediterranean trade routes and coastal territories.
events.certainty_note: Byzantine-Arab naval conflicts are attested in Byzantine chronicles, but specific engagements in 757 CE are often undated or conflated with broader campaigns.

disconfirming_evidence: An Lushan's death date is sometimes placed in late 756 CE rather than 757 CE in some sources, though 757 CE is the scholarly consensus. No other major disconfirming evidence identified for events listed.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 757 CE is moderate, primarily due to the well-attested death of An Lushan. Chinese chronicles provide detailed coverage of the An Lushan Rebellion and its leadership transition. Islamic sources offer general accounts of Abbasid consolidation but rarely date specific events to individual years. Frankish and Byzantine sources are sparser for this year. The record is heavily weighted toward military and political history; economic, social, and cultural developments are largely absent.

graph_edges.note: An Lushan's death and the transition to An Qingxu's leadership eventually led to the rebellion's suppression in 763 CE
Abbasid consolidation continued throughout al-Mansur's reign, culminating in the founding of Baghdad
Pepin III's military campaigns established the foundation for Charlemagne's expansionist policies
